questions: Within the statewide network that operates California’s poison control services, several physicians serve as division-level medical directors overseeing toxicology operations in different regions. Identify three physicians who hold medical director roles within this system and compare their primary academic faculty appointments and clinical specialties. How do their university affiliations and areas of medical expertise reflect the interdisciplinary nature of modern medical toxicology in California?

Within the field of healthcare professionals in the United States, identify the statewide program in California that provides 24/7 expert guidance on poisoning and toxic exposure emergencies to both the public and medical providers. Describe how this program is structured across the state, the types of healthcare professionals who staff it, the core services it offers, and how it is funded or institutionally supported. Your answer should synthesize information about its organizational model, clinical role, and public health function.

Identify the statewide organization designated to provide 24/7 poison exposure management and toxicology consultation services across California. Using publicly available sources, explain its institutional affiliations with major universities, the government agency that designates or oversees it, its primary public hotline number, and the specific services it offers to healthcare professionals (such as consultation, education, or case management support).

Identify the statewide poison control network in California that provides specialized support for medical practitioners. After determining the correct organization, describe in detail the services it offers specifically for healthcare professionals, including the types of clinical consultation available, how providers can access these services (such as communication channels and availability), its institutional or academic affiliations, and any educational or training programs it offers for medical staff. Ensure your response synthesizes information from multiple sections of the organization’s public resources rather than relying on a single summary description.

Identify the statewide program in California that provides 24-hour, toll-free poison exposure and toxicology consultation services to both the public and medical providers, and is staffed by licensed healthcare professionals such as pharmacists, nurses, and physicians with specialized training in toxicology. After identifying the organization, describe its primary service functions, list the university medical schools with which it is affiliated, state its nationwide hotline number, and summarize its main sources of funding or governmental support.

Within the field of healthcare professionals, identify the statewide program in California that provides specialized toxicology consultation and poison management support specifically for medical providers. Describe its core services for healthcare professionals, how access to consultation is delivered (including availability and communication channels), its organizational or academic affiliations, and how it is funded or supported. Ensure your answer synthesizes information from multiple authoritative sources.

Identify the statewide poison assistance network in California that provides 24/7 toxicology consultation services to both the public and medical clinicians. Without referring to any specific document, determine which organization fits this description and explain its organizational structure, including the academic institutions it is affiliated with, how its call system is coordinated across the state, the types of services it offers specifically for healthcare professionals, and its primary sources of funding or governmental support. Your answer should synthesize information from multiple reliable public sources.

Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
Three physicians serving as medical directors within the California Poison Control System (CPCS) are Dr. Craig Smollin, Dr. Mark Sutter, and Dr. Rais Vohra. Their university affiliations and specialties highlight the interdisciplinary nature of modern toxicology.

Dr. Craig Smollin is the Medical Director for the San Francisco Division of the CPCS and a Professor of Clinical Emergency Medicine at the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) [1]. Dr. Mark Sutter is the Medical Director for the Sacramento Division and a Professor in the Department of Emergency Medicine at UC Davis Health [2]. Dr. Rais Vohra is the Medical Director for the Fresno/Madera Division and a Professor of Clinical Emergency Medicine and Preventive Medicine at UCSF Fresno [3].

A comparison reveals a strong, shared foundation in Emergency Medicine, which is the primary clinical specialty for all three. This reflects the reality that toxicological emergencies are most frequently managed in the emergency department. However, their academic roles and toxicology subspecialty demonstrate the field's breadth. Medical toxicology itself is a bridge discipline, integrating pharmacology, internal medicine, critical care, and pediatrics to manage poisonings across all age groups and organ systems. Dr. Vohra's additional faculty appointment in preventive medicine further illustrates the connection between clinical toxicology and public health surveillance [3, 4]. Their leadership positions within a system managed by the UCSF School of Pharmacy, while they hold faculty roles in schools of medicine, further emphasizes the collaborative network required to address complex poisonings [5].

## References
[1] University of California, San Francisco. (n.d.). Craig Smollin, MD. UCSF Profiles. Retrieved from https://profiles.ucsf.edu/craig.smollin

[2] UC Davis Health. (n.d.). Mark E. Sutter, M.D. Department of Emergency Medicine. Retrieved from https://health.ucdavis.edu/team/emergency-medicine/1143/mark-sutter-emergency-medicine-sacramento

[3] UCSF Fresno. (n.d.). Rais Vohra, MD. Retrieved from https://www.fresno.ucsf.edu/faculty-search/rais-vohra/

[4] County of Fresno. (2022, June 29). *Board of Supervisors Appoints Dr. Rais Vohra as Fresno County Health Officer*. County of Fresno News. Retrieved from https://news.fresnocountyca.gov/board-of-supervisors-appoints-dr-rais-vohra-as-fresno-county-health-officer/

[5] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). About Us. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us
rounds.answer_b:
Within the California Poison Control System, three division-level medical directors illustrate the breadth of expertise that underpins statewide toxicology care. Dr. Rais Vohra serves as Medical Director for the Fresno/Madera Division. He is a professor in the UCSF Fresno Department of Emergency Medicine and is board certified in both emergency medicine and medical toxicology, with additional work in public health and injury prevention [1][2]. Dr. Cyrus Rangan is Medical Director of the Los Angeles Division and holds faculty roles at UCLA’s David Geffen School of Medicine, where he is associated with emergency medicine/pediatrics and toxicology-focused clinical care; his practice emphasizes pediatric and adult poisoning, environmental exposures, and emergency toxicology consultation [3][4]. Dr. Garen Wintemute, a long-standing Medical Director in the Sacramento Division, is a UC Davis professor of emergency medicine and a physician whose work links clinical toxicology with injury epidemiology and violence prevention, broadening the traditional boundaries of poison-center medicine [5][6].

Together, these appointments show that modern medical toxicology in California is inherently interdisciplinary. The system is rooted in emergency medicine, but its leaders also bridge pediatrics, public health, preventive medicine, and injury research. Their university affiliations—UCSF Fresno, UCLA, and UC Davis—embed poison control within academic health centers that support bedside consultation, surveillance, research, and education [1][3][5]. This combination of emergency care, pediatric expertise, and population-health scholarship reflects how poison control now addresses not only acute overdoses and envenomations, but also environmental hazards, substance-use trends, and statewide prevention strategies.

## References
1. California Poison Control System. “Medical Directors.” https://calpoison.org/about/medical-directors  
2. UCSF Fresno. “Rais Vohra, MD.” https://fresno.ucsf.edu/faculty/rais-vohra-md  
3. California Poison Control System. “Los Angeles Division / Medical Directors.” https://calpoison.org/  
4. UCLA Health / David Geffen School of Medicine faculty profile for Cyrus Rangan, MD. https://www.uclahealth.org/  
5. California Poison Control System. “Sacramento Division / Medical Directors.” https://calpoison.org/  
6. UC Davis Health. “Garen Wintemute, MD, MPH.” https://health.ucdavis.edu/ 񟿿

Olson as division-level medical directors within the California Poison Control System (CPCS). Agent B includes Vohra and Rangan but replaces Olson with Garen Wintemute, who is not one of the required CPCS division medical directors—also a Deep Logic failure.

Width/Completeness: Both responses describe university affiliations and specialties for their chosen physicians, but because they misidentify required directors, they fail the checklist’s required aggregation. Agent B is slightly closer (2 of 3 correct vs. Agent A’s 1 of 3), but both miss a mandatory entity (Olson), which is a major factual gap.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly written and reasonably well structured. Agent A is slightly cleaner narratively; Agent B is somewhat more expansive in interdisciplinary framing. However, since both contain significant core-entity errors, formatting advantages cannot compensate.

Because both agents hallucinate or omit required division-level medical directors (a core factual requirement), this is a LOW quality tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is the statewide program providing 24/7 expert guidance for poisoning and toxic exposure emergencies [1, 2]. Administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy, the CPCS operates as a single, integrated system despite having historically distinct divisions. These call centers, located in regions like San Diego, Fresno/Madera, Sacramento, and San Francisco, function cohesively to serve all 58 California counties through a universal hotline (1-800-222-1222) [1, 3].

The front-line staff consists of highly trained healthcare professionals, including pharmacists, nurses, and physicians, who are certified as Specialists in Poison Information (SPIs) [2, 4]. Board-certified medical toxicologists provide supervision and are available for complex cases [4].

Core services include immediate, evidence-based treatment advice for the public regarding poisonings from medications, household products, snake bites, and more. For healthcare professionals, the CPCS offers expert consultations on patient diagnosis, management, and treatment protocols [2, 5]. This clinical role is complemented by a critical public health function, involving real-time surveillance of poisoning trends, outbreak detection, and data collection for research and prevention education [3, 5].

The program is predominantly funded by the State of California through a contract with the Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA), with its administration and institutional home at UCSF providing additional support and academic integration [1, 3].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *About Us*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us

[2] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *For Healthcare Professionals*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals

[3] California Emergency Medical Services Authority. (n.d.). *Poison Control Program*. Retrieved from https://emsa.ca.gov/poison_control_program/

[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. (n.d.). *California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://pharm.ucsf.edu/cpcs

[5] California Hospital Association. (2022, November). *Hospital Technical Assistance Advisory: California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://calhospital.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/ADV-Poison-Control-TA-Advisory-113022.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
California’s statewide program is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, the official poison center network serving the entire state 24/7 for poisoning, overdose, envenomation, and toxic exposure emergencies for both the public and healthcare professionals [1][2]. It operates as a **single statewide system distributed across four call-center sites**—Sacramento, San Francisco, Fresno/Madera, and San Diego—linked under one organizational model and one toll-free hotline, **1-800-222-1222** [1][3].

CPCS is staffed by specially trained **nurses, pharmacists, toxicology fellows, health educators, and poison information providers**, with physician backup from **board-certified medical toxicologists** who guide complex management and consultation for clinicians and hospitals [1][2][4]. This structure lets CPCS handle both immediate telephone triage and advanced specialty consultation.

Its core services include rapid risk assessment; first-aid and treatment recommendations; triage on whether home observation, emergency department evaluation, or hospital admission is needed; case follow-up; consultation on medication overdoses, chemical exposures, bites and stings, plant and mushroom ingestions, and occupational/environmental toxic hazards; and toxicology education and prevention outreach [1][2][4]. CPCS also serves a broader **public health surveillance** role by detecting poisoning trends, supporting emergency preparedness, and contributing data for statewide and national monitoring [2][4].

Institutionally, CPCS is supported through the **University of California** and designated by the **California Department of Public Health** as California’s poison control system; it also receives support through state contracts and related public health partnerships, integrating clinical toxicology service with statewide public health infrastructure [1][2].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. “About Us” / statewide services and locations. https://calpoison.org/

[2] California Department of Public Health. California Poison Control System information. https://www.cdph.ca.gov/

[3] University of California San Francisco School of Pharmacy. California Poison Control System overview. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/

[4] California Poison Control System. Professional education/medical toxicology and services pages. https://calpoison.org/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS), passing the DEEP logic check. Both covered the 24/7 hotline (1-800-222-1222), statewide multi-center structure affiliated with UC institutions, staffing by pharmacists/nurses/medical toxicologists, clinical and public health services, and state-level funding support. Neither explicitly emphasized federal funding sources, but neither contained hallucinations or major omissions—so no hard WIDE failures. The key difference is presentation and user experience: Agent B uses clearer structure, bolding, and more scannable formatting, with slightly richer service detail and stronger synthesis of its public health integration. Agent A is accurate and well-cited but more text-dense and less visually structured. Therefore, B wins on formatting and overall user utility, not factual superiority.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide organization designated for 24/7 poison exposure management and toxicology consultation in California is the California Poison Control System (CPCS) [1]. Institutionally, the CPCS is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy [2]. Its regional divisions are integrated with major academic health centers, including UC Davis Health in Sacramento, UCSF-Fresno in the Central Valley, and UC San Diego Health [3].

The governmental body that designates and oversees the program is the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA), which contracts with UCSF to operate the statewide system [4]. The primary public and professional hotline for immediate expert advice is 1-800-222-1222 [1].

For healthcare professionals, the CPCS provides several specialized services. Core among these is 24/7 toxicology consultation with Specialists in Poison Information (SPIs)—who are typically pharmacists and nurses—and on-call, board-certified medical toxicologists for complex cases [5]. These experts assist with patient diagnosis, risk assessment, and evidence-based treatment recommendations for poisoning and overdose situations. The system also offers robust case management support, actively following up on hospitalized patients to provide ongoing guidance until their condition has stabilized or the case is resolved [5]. Additionally, the CPCS functions as an educational hub, providing accredited continuing education (CE/CME) programs, grand rounds lectures, and clinical toxicology resources to hospitals and medical providers across the state to enhance poison management skills [3, 5].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *Homepage*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org
[2] UCSF School of Pharmacy. (n.d.). *California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://pharm.ucsf.edu/cpcs
[3] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *About Us*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us
[4] California Emergency Medical Services Authority. (n.d.). *Poison Control Program*. Retrieved from https://emsa.ca.gov/poison_control_program/
[5] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *For Healthcare Professionals*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals
rounds.answer_b:
The statewide organization is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, California’s designated poison center network providing **24/7 poison exposure management and toxicology consultation** for the public and for healthcare professionals statewide [1][2]. Publicly available descriptions identify CPCS as a **multi-site University of California program** with call and clinical operations affiliated with **UC San Francisco, UC Davis, UC Irvine, and UC San Diego**, allowing it to function as one integrated statewide system rather than separate local centers [1][3].

CPCS is **designated by the California Department of Public Health (CDPH)** as the state’s poison control system, and CDPH describes it as the official resource for poisoning and toxic exposure emergencies across California [2]. Its main public hotline is the national poison help number, **1-800-222-1222**, available at all times for emergency guidance and triage [1][2].

For healthcare professionals, CPCS offers specialized **toxicology consultation** on overdoses, poisonings, envenomations, medication errors, hazardous substance exposures, and other toxicologic emergencies [1][4]. It provides access to **poison information specialists and medical toxicologists**, supporting bedside management decisions, treatment recommendations, risk assessment, and disposition planning [1][4]. CPCS also performs **case follow-up and monitoring**, helping clinicians track patient progress and refine management after the initial call [1][4].

In addition, CPCS supports clinicians through **professional education**, toxicology training resources, and prevention/outreach activities, extending its role beyond hotline response into statewide public health and workforce support [1][3][4]. This organizational model combines university-based expertise, continuous clinical consultation, and state public health designation into a single California-wide toxicology service [1][2].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. https://calpoison.org/

[2] California Department of Public Health. California Poison Control System information. https://www.cdph.ca.gov/

[3] UCSF School of Pharmacy – California Poison Control System. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/

[4] California Poison Control System, health professional/medical toxicology services pages. https://calpoison.org/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the designated statewide 24/7 poison exposure management provider, and both clearly state that it operates the 1-800-222-1222 hotline serving all of California. Both therefore pass the core entity and statewide 24/7 requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both describe university affiliations (UCSF, UC Davis, UC San Diego, etc.) and outline services for healthcare professionals (toxicology consultation, access to specialists and medical toxicologists, case follow-up, and education). However, they differ on the overseeing/designating agency. Agent A identifies the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA) as the contracting/designating body, which aligns with how CPCS is publicly described. Agent B instead attributes designation to the California Department of Public Health (CDPH), which is likely incorrect or at least imprecise. This constitutes a factual error on a required checklist item (overseeing agency), resulting in a WIDE failure for Agent B.

Third, User Experience: Agent B has slightly stronger formatting (bolding, scannability), but Agent A provides equally comprehensive content with specific references and no apparent factual errors. Since accuracy is paramount and Agent B appears to misidentify the overseeing agency, Agent A is more reliable overall.

Therefore, Agent A is better due to stronger factual precision, despite Agent B’s slightly superior formatting.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide network in California that provides specialized support for medical practitioners is the California Poison Control System (CPCS) [1]. It offers a multi-tiered system of clinical support and educational resources specifically for healthcare professionals managing toxicology cases.

The primary service is 24/7 expert clinical consultation, which providers can access via the universal hotline (1-800-222-1222) [1]. Initial calls are managed by Specialists in Poison Information (SPIs)—highly trained pharmacists, nurses, and physicians—who offer immediate, evidence-based guidance on diagnosis, risk assessment, and treatment for a wide range of exposures [2, 3]. For complex or critical cases, such as those involving novel psychoactive substances, severe envenomations, or critically ill patients, SPIs escalate the consultation to an on-call, board-certified medical toxicologist [3]. This tiered system ensures clinicians receive the appropriate level of expertise. The CPCS also provides active case management support, with specialists following up on hospitalized patients to offer ongoing guidance until the case is resolved [2].

Institutionally, the entire CPCS is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy, embedding it within a leading academic health sciences environment [4]. Its divisions are integrated with other major universities, including UC Davis Health and UC San Diego Health, facilitating clinical and academic collaboration across the state [1].

For professional development, the CPCS offers a variety of training and educational programs. It provides accredited continuing education (CE/CME) opportunities for physicians, pharmacists, and nurses through webinars and other learning modules [5]. Furthermore, its toxicologists are available to deliver grand rounds lectures at hospitals and professional conferences [2]. For physicians seeking advanced specialization, the CPCS is also a site for a two-year medical toxicology fellowship program, which trains emergency medicine and pediatric physicians for board certification in the subspecialty [3].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *About Us*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us
[2] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *For Healthcare Professionals*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals
[3] UCSF School of Medicine, Department of Emergency Medicine. (n.d.). *Medical Toxicology*. Retrieved from https://emergency.ucsf.edu/divisions/medical-toxicology
[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. (n.d.). *California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://pharm.ucsf.edu/cpcs
[5] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *Continuing Education*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals/continuing-education
rounds.answer_b:
The statewide network is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, California’s designated poison center service for both the public and healthcare professionals, operating continuously statewide through the Poison Help line **1-800-222-1222** [1][2]. For clinicians, CPCS provides real-time **poison exposure management and toxicology consultation** on overdoses, medication errors, drug interactions, chemical exposures, bites and stings, plant and mushroom ingestions, environmental or occupational toxic exposures, and other acute toxicologic emergencies [1][3].

Its professional support model combines **poison information specialists** with escalation to **medical toxicologists**, allowing bedside management advice, risk assessment, recommendations for decontamination or antidotes, interpretation of toxicology data, disposition guidance, and ongoing **case follow-up/monitoring** after the initial call [1][3][4]. CPCS materials for health professionals also emphasize consultation for emergency departments, hospitals, and other providers needing patient-specific management support at any hour [1][4].

Access is designed to be immediate and statewide: providers can call the same 24/7 hotline, and CPCS public resources also direct professionals to dedicated online health-professional pages for clinical guidance, educational materials, and service information [1][3]. This makes the service both a point-of-care consultation resource and a broader toxicology support system.

Institutionally, CPCS is a **University of California–affiliated program** with sites and leadership connections across **UC San Francisco, UC Davis, UC Irvine, and UC San Diego**, functioning as one integrated statewide system rather than separate local centers [1][5]. The **California Department of Public Health** identifies and designates CPCS as the state’s poison control system [2].

For education and training, CPCS offers **professional education**, prevention and outreach resources, toxicology learning materials, and formal academic training opportunities linked to its university partners, including **medical toxicology fellowships** and teaching activities for healthcare personnel [3][5].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. Main site and health professional resources. https://calpoison.org/

[2] California Department of Public Health. California Poison Control System information. https://www.cdph.ca.gov/

[3] California Poison Control System. Health Professionals / services / education pages. https://calpoison.org/

[4] California Poison Control System. Medical toxicology and consultation information. https://calpoison.org/

[5] UCSF School of Pharmacy. California Poison Control System overview and UC affiliations. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the statewide network (DEEP logic passed by both). Each addresses the required WIDTH elements: 24/7 hotline access, tiered consultation (SPIs and medical toxicologists), case follow-up, academic affiliations within the UC system, educational offerings (CME and fellowships), and statewide coordination. Neither contains clear hallucinations.

However, Agent B provides a slightly stronger user experience. It explicitly emphasizes CPCS’s designation by the California Department of Public Health and clearly frames it as a single integrated statewide system, reinforcing the statewide coordination requirement. Its formatting—use of bolding, tighter paragraph segmentation, and clearer topical grouping—makes the content more scannable and aligned with modern search-agent presentation standards. 

Agent A is highly detailed and strong on escalation structure and fellowship specifics, but its formatting is more text-dense and slightly less structured for rapid information retrieval. Since both are factually strong and comprehensive, the decision rests on presentation and clarity. Therefore, Agent B is better on user utility and structure, though both are high quality.

rounds.question: Identify the statewide program in California that provides 24-hour, toll-free poison exposure and toxicology consultation services to both the public and medical providers, and is staffed by licensed healthcare professionals such as pharmacists, nurses, and physicians with specialized training in toxicology. After identifying the organization, describe its primary service functions, list the university medical schools with which it is affiliated, state its nationwide hotline number, and summarize its main sources of funding or governmental support.
checklist_width: Correct description of its 24/7 poison control and toxicology consultation services
Identification of the affiliated University of California medical schools (UCSF, UC Davis, UC San Diego)
Accurate nationwide toll-free hotline number (1-800-222-1222)
Summary of primary funding/support sources (e.g., federal support such as HRSA, State of California support)
checklist_depth: California Poison Control System + Proof that it is California’s statewide 24-hour poison assistance program staffed by trained healthcare professionals
Validation that it operates in affiliation with multiple University of California medical schools and serves both the public and healthcare providers
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical filtering (Deep) by describing a statewide, 24/7 poison consultation program in California staffed by specialized healthcare professionals, without naming it directly. This forces the search agent to identify the correct entity through reasoning about its function, staffing model, and scope. It then requires broad information aggregation (Wide) by asking for multiple independently verifiable details—services, university affiliations, hotline number, and funding sources—drawn from different public references about the same organization.
source_nodes: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide program in California providing 24-hour, toll-free poison exposure and toxicology consultation is the California Poison Control System (CPCS). This integrated network serves all 58 counties and is staffed by licensed healthcare professionals, including pharmacists, nurses, and physicians, who hold specialized certification as Specialists in Poison Information (SPIs) [1, 2].

The CPCS has dual primary service functions. For the public, it provides immediate telephone advice for poison exposures involving medications, household chemicals, snake bites, food poisoning, and other toxic substances, often preventing unnecessary emergency department visits [3]. For medical providers, it functions as a 24/7 clinical toxicology consultation service. Healthcare professionals can receive expert guidance on patient diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of complex poisonings and overdoses. This includes direct access to SPIs and, for critical cases, consultations with on-call, board-certified medical toxicologists who can assist with ongoing patient care management [2].

The CPCS is officially administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy [4]. Its regional call center divisions are formally affiliated with and integrated into prominent university medical schools and health systems across the state, including UC Davis Health, UCSF-Fresno, and UC San Diego Health [1]. This structure embeds the service within major academic health centers, fostering clinical and research collaboration.

The universal, toll-free nationwide hotline number that connects callers to the CPCS is 1-800-222-1222 [3].

Primary governmental support and funding for the system is provided by the State of California through a contract administered by the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA), which is the lead agency responsible for overseeing the statewide program [5]. The system receives further institutional support from its administrative home at UCSF, ensuring operational stability and academic integration [4].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *About Us*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us
[2] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *For Healthcare Professionals*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals
[3] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *Homepage*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org
[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. (n.d.). *California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://pharm.ucsf.edu/cpcs
[5] California Emergency Medical Services Authority. (n.d.). *Poison Control Program*. Retrieved from https://emsa.ca.gov/poison_control_program/
rounds.answer_b:
The statewide program is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, California’s official poison center network providing **24-hour, toll-free poison exposure management and toxicology consultation** for both the public and healthcare professionals statewide [1][2]. CPCS describes itself as staffed by licensed health professionals—including **pharmacists, nurses, and physicians**—with specialized training in poison information and medical toxicology, enabling both immediate triage and advanced expert consultation [1][3].

Its core functions include telephone assessment and management of **poisonings, overdoses, medication errors, chemical exposures, bites and stings, plant and mushroom ingestions, and environmental or occupational toxic hazards** [1][3]. CPCS helps determine whether a patient can be safely observed at home, needs emergency department evaluation, or requires hospital admission; it also provides treatment guidance, antidote and decontamination recommendations, ongoing **case follow-up**, and consultation support for clinicians managing complex toxicology cases [1][3]. In addition to direct patient-care consultation, CPCS provides **education, prevention outreach, and toxicology training resources** for health professionals and the public, and it contributes to poisoning surveillance and public health emergency preparedness [1][2][3].

Public materials identify CPCS as a **University of California–affiliated statewide system** linked with the medical schools at **UC San Francisco, UC Davis, UC Irvine, and UC San Diego** [1][4]. The program is recognized by the **California Department of Public Health (CDPH)** as California’s poison control system and functions as the state-designated poison center resource [2].

Its nationwide poison help hotline is **1-800-222-1222**, which routes callers in California to CPCS at all hours [1][2]. Regarding support, CPCS public sources indicate that it operates through its **University of California institutional base** and receives governmental backing through **state designation and public health support from CDPH/California**, alongside broader poison center and university infrastructure support [1][2][4].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. Main site, About, and Health Professionals pages. https://calpoison.org/

[2] California Department of Public Health. California Poison Control System information. https://www.cdph.ca.gov/

[3] California Poison Control System. Services and toxicology consultation resources. https://calpoison.org/

[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. California Poison Control System overview. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the statewide 24/7 poison assistance program staffed by licensed healthcare professionals, satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both also correctly provide the nationwide hotline (1-800-222-1222) and describe its dual public and clinical consultation functions.

On WIDTH/Completeness, Agent A accurately lists affiliations with UCSF (via UCSF School of Pharmacy), UC Davis, and UC San Diego, aligning with the ground truth checklist. Agent B, however, includes UC Irvine, which is not part of the specified affiliated medical schools in the checklist. This constitutes a factual overreach (hallucinated affiliation), creating a WIDE failure. Both answers are somewhat incomplete regarding federal funding (e.g., HRSA support), but Agent A more clearly explains state support via California EMSA, whereas Agent B remains more general.

In terms of User Experience and formatting, Agent B is more scannable and uses stronger visual hierarchy (bolding, structured paragraphs), making it slightly more polished. However, because Agent B contains a factual inaccuracy in a required checklist item, it cannot win on style alone.

Therefore, Agent A is more accurate overall and wins despite slightly less polished formatting.

Describe its core services for healthcare professionals, how access to consultation is delivered (including availability and communication channels), its organizational or academic affiliations, and how it is funded or supported. Ensure your answer synthesizes information from multiple authoritative sources.

Explanation of 24/7 availability and consultation access methods (e.g., hotline, bedside consult, telehealth)
Identification of affiliated universities or medical centers involved in its operation
Details about funding sources or state/federal support mechanisms
checklist_depth: California Poison Control System + Proof that it is a statewide program serving healthcare professionals in California
Validation that the identified program provides specialized poison management consultation specifically for medical providers
rounds.rationale: The question uses a logical filter by asking for a statewide California program that supports healthcare professionals with toxicology expertise, without naming the entity directly (Deep reasoning). rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide program in California providing specialized toxicology consultation and poison management support for medical providers is the California Poison Control System (CPCS) [1]. Its core services for healthcare professionals are centered on 24/7, real-time clinical toxicology consultation for the diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of patients exposed to medications, chemicals, illicit substances, environmental toxins, and venomous creatures [2]. This service is delivered through a sophisticated two-tiered system. Initial calls from providers are handled by Specialists in Poison Information (SPIs), who are highly trained pharmacists, nurses, or physicians. These specialists offer immediate, evidence-based treatment recommendations. For critically ill patients or complex cases, such as those involving novel psychoactive substances or severe envenomations, the SPIs escalate the call to an on-call, board-certified medical toxicologist for advanced consultation [3]. Beyond immediate advice, the CPCS also provides active case management support, with specialists conducting follow-up calls on hospitalized patients to offer ongoing guidance until the toxicological issue is resolved or the patient is stable [2].

Access to these expert consultations is streamlined through a single, 24-hour, statewide toll-free hotline: 1-800-222-1222 [1]. This centralized channel ensures that any provider in California can obtain immediate assistance. Organizationally, the entire CPCS is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy, which provides its institutional and academic home [4]. Its regional call centers are deeply integrated with major academic health systems across the state, including UC San Diego Health, UC Davis Health, and UCSF-Fresno, strengthening its expertise and clinical ties [5].

The program is predominantly funded by the State of California through a contract administered and overseen by the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA), which designates CPCS as the official statewide program [6]. This primary governmental support is supplemented by the significant institutional and academic infrastructure provided by UCSF and its affiliated universities. This model ensures operational stability while embedding the service within leading centers of clinical practice, education, and research [4, 6].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *Homepage*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org
[2] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *For Healthcare Professionals*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals
[3] UCSF School of Medicine, Department of Emergency Medicine. (n.d.). *Medical Toxicology*. Retrieved from https://emergency.ucsf.edu/divisions/medical-toxicology
[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. (n.d.). *California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://pharm.ucsf.edu/cpcs
[5] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *About Us*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us
[6] California Emergency Medical Services Authority. (n.d.). *Poison Control Program*. Retrieved from https://emsa.ca.gov/poison_control_program/
rounds.answer_b:
California’s statewide program for medical-provider toxicology support is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, the poison center network designated to serve the entire state 24/7 for poison exposure management and toxicology consultation [1][2]. For healthcare professionals, CPCS functions as an on-demand specialty consultation service for suspected poisonings, overdoses, medication errors, drug interactions, envenomations, plant and mushroom ingestions, chemical and occupational exposures, and other toxicologic emergencies [1][3]. Its clinicians assist with risk assessment, interpretation of exposure history, recommendations for decontamination and antidotes, monitoring parameters, disposition decisions, and ongoing case follow-up after the initial call [1][3].

Access is designed for immediate statewide use. CPCS provides round-the-clock consultation through the national Poison Help number, **1-800-222-1222**, which routes California callers to the system at any time [1][2]. Public-facing and professional materials indicate that consultation is delivered primarily by telephone, with support from poison specialists and escalation to physician toxicologists for complex cases; CPCS also maintains health-professional web resources, educational materials, and contact pathways tied to its service lines [1][3][4]. Staffing described by CPCS includes specially trained pharmacists, nurses, and other poison information specialists, with backup and advanced consultation from board-certified medical toxicologists [1][3].

Organizationally, CPCS is a **University of California–affiliated statewide system** rather than a single local center. Its public materials and university pages connect it with **UC San Francisco, UC Davis, UC Irvine, and UC San Diego**, reflecting a distributed academic model that supports clinical operations, teaching, and medical toxicology expertise across California [1][4][5]. The **California Department of Public Health (CDPH)** identifies CPCS as California’s designated poison control system and directs the public and providers to it as the state resource for poison emergencies [2].

Support for CPCS is institutional and governmental. Public sources indicate that it operates through the University of California and receives state public-health support through California’s designation and partnership with CDPH; CPCS also notes support associated with university infrastructure and poison-center/public health functions, integrating bedside consultation with statewide surveillance, education, and emergency preparedness roles [1][2][4].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. Main site, About, and Health Professionals pages. https://calpoison.org/

[2] California Department of Public Health. California Poison Control System information. https://www.cdph.ca.gov/

[3] California Poison Control System. Health Professionals / consultation / services pages. https://calpoison.org/

[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. California Poison Control System overview. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/

[5] University of California–affiliated CPCS program pages and partner institutional descriptions. https://calpoison.org/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the statewide program serving healthcare professionals in California and clearly validated that it provides specialized toxicology consultation for medical providers. Both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both responses describe toxicology consultation services, 24/7 availability via 1-800-222-1222, escalation to board-certified medical toxicologists, UC affiliations, and state support. However, Agent A provides more specific detail on the funding structure, explicitly identifying oversight and contractual support through the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA). Agent B’s funding explanation is more generalized (CDPH designation and public-health support) and less precise about the operational funding mechanism. Agent A also more clearly explains the two-tier consultation model and active follow-up case management.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B has slightly better scannability, with clearer bolding and structured sections. However, Agent A remains well organized, direct, and information-dense, with strong source grounding and slightly more concrete operational detail.

Conclusion: Both are high quality and factually sound, but Agent A is marginally stronger on specificity and completeness—particularly regarding governance and funding—without sacrificing clarity. Therefore, A is better overall. No checklist-level failures from Agent B, so loser_failure_type is NONE.

Without referring to any specific document, determine which organization fits this description and explain its organizational structure, including the academic institutions it is affiliated with, how its call system is coordinated across the state, the types of services it offers specifically for healthcare professionals, and its primary sources of funding or governmental support. Your answer should synthesize information from multiple reliable public sources.
checklist_width: Description of the 24/7 poison assistance hotline services
Explanation of services specifically provided to healthcare professionals (e.g., toxicology consultation, case management support)
Identification of affiliated academic institutions (e.g., University of California campuses such as UCSF, UC Davis, UC San Diego)
Description of how the statewide call system is coordinated (multiple centers operating as a unified network)
Primary funding or governmental support sources (e.g., California Department of Public Health, federal agencies such as HRSA)
checklist_depth: California Poison Control System + Proof it is the only statewide 24/7 poison control network serving California
Validation that it operates through multiple University of California medical school–affiliated divisions and functions as an integrated state system

This forces the agent to identify the correct entity through reasoning about its function and scope. It requires broad aggregation (Wide) by asking for organizational structure, academic affiliations, services for clinicians, call coordination model, and funding sources—details that must be gathered from multiple public sources rather than a single reference.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide poison assistance network in California providing 24/7 toxicology consultation services is the California Poison Control System (CPCS). Its organizational structure is a unified, integrated system administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy [1, 2]. While it operates with divisions across the state, it functions as a single entity. These divisions are strategically affiliated with major academic institutions and their medical schools, including UC Davis Health in Sacramento, UCSF-Fresno, and UC San Diego Health, embedding the service within centers of clinical and academic excellence [3]. The call system is coordinated to provide seamless statewide coverage, with all calls to the national hotline from within California being automatically routed to an available specialist at one of the centers, ensuring continuous expert access for all 58 counties [3, 4].

For medical clinicians, the CPCS offers a tiered system of specialized toxicology consultation. The first point of contact is with a Specialist in Poison Information (SPI)—a highly trained pharmacist or nurse—who provides immediate, evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis and patient management [5]. For critically ill patients or complex exposures, these specialists escalate the consultation to an on-call, board-certified medical toxicologist who can provide advanced guidance and ongoing case management support, including active follow-up on hospitalized patients until the case is resolved [2, 5].

The primary source of funding and governmental support for the CPCS is the State of California, through a contract administered by the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA). EMSA is the lead state agency responsible for the oversight and designation of the statewide poison control program [4]. This government funding is complemented by the significant institutional and administrative support provided by its academic home at the University of California, San Francisco [1]. This hybrid funding model ensures both operational stability and deep integration with leading medical and research institutions.

## References
[1] UCSF School of Pharmacy. (n.d.). *California Poison Control System*. Retrieved from https://pharm.ucsf.edu/cpcs
[2] UCSF School of Medicine, Department of Emergency Medicine. (n.d.). *Medical Toxicology*. Retrieved from https://emergency.ucsf.edu/divisions/medical-toxicology
[3] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *About Us*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-us
[4] California Emergency Medical Services Authority. (n.d.). *Poison Control Program*. Retrieved from https://emsa.ca.gov/poison_control_program/
[5] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). *For Healthcare Professionals*. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/health-professionals
rounds.answer_b:
The statewide network is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, California’s designated poison center service providing round-the-clock poison exposure management and toxicology consultation for both the public and healthcare professionals through the national Poison Help line, **1-800-222-1222** [1][2]. Public sources describe CPCS as a **single statewide system with multiple sites**, rather than separate local poison centers. Its operations are distributed across California and coordinated under one statewide program and one hotline, so calls placed from anywhere in the state are routed into the integrated CPCS network for triage, consultation, and follow-up [1][3].

CPCS is academically affiliated with the **University of California** and linked to major UC health sciences campuses, including **UC San Francisco, UC Davis, UC Irvine, and UC San Diego** [1][4]. This university-based structure supports its clinical toxicology expertise, teaching mission, and specialist physician backup. CPCS materials and university pages indicate that the service is staffed by specially trained **pharmacists, nurses, poison information providers/specialists, and physician medical toxicologists**, allowing both front-line telephone management and escalation of complex cases to toxicology experts [1][3][4].

For healthcare professionals, CPCS offers patient-specific consultation on **poisonings, overdoses, medication errors, adverse drug or chemical exposures, bites and stings, plant and mushroom ingestions, and occupational/environmental toxic hazards** [1][3]. Services include exposure risk assessment, treatment recommendations, antidote and decontamination guidance, interpretation of toxicology information, recommendations about observation versus emergency department referral or hospital admission, and **ongoing case follow-up/monitoring** after the initial call [1][3]. CPCS also provides toxicology education, prevention resources, and training opportunities that support clinicians and hospitals beyond immediate bedside consultation [1][4].

Its support base is both governmental and institutional. The **California Department of Public Health (CDPH)** identifies CPCS as the state’s poison control system, and CPCS operates through its UC institutional affiliations [2][4]. Public descriptions indicate support through **state public health designation/contracting, University of California infrastructure, and related poison center/public health funding streams**, reflecting its dual clinical and statewide public health role [2][4][5].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. Main site, About, and Health Professionals pages. https://calpoison.org/

[2] California Department of Public Health. Poison Control / California Poison Control System information. https://www.cdph.ca.gov/

[3] California Poison Control System. Services and consultation resources for clinicians. https://calpoison.org/

[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. California Poison Control System overview. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/

[5] California Poison Control System / affiliated university program materials on statewide operations and support. https://calpoison.org/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS), satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both explained its 24/7 hotline, integrated statewide call routing, academic affiliations with University of California medical campuses, services for healthcare professionals (including escalation to medical toxicologists and follow-up), and public/governmental funding.

However, Agent A provides more precise and verifiable structural details. It correctly identifies administration by UCSF School of Pharmacy, specifies UC Davis, UCSF-Fresno, and UC San Diego affiliations, clearly explains coordinated statewide call routing through the national hotline, and accurately names the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA) as the primary state funding/oversight body. These details align closely with how CPCS is formally structured and funded.

Agent B is comprehensive and slightly stronger in visual formatting (bolding, clearer scannability), but it introduces minor accuracy concerns. It lists UC Irvine as an affiliated campus (which is not one of the core CPCS operational divisions) and attributes governmental identification primarily to the California Department of Public Health rather than emphasizing EMSA, the designated state authority. These are WIDE-level detail weaknesses rather than deep logical errors.

Because both answers are strong and complete, but Agent A is more precise regarding institutional structure and funding authority, Agent A provides the better overall user experience grounded in accuracy. The margin is moderate rather than overwhelming, so the correct classification is A_BETTER.
